coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the properties of electrospun polyvinyl Alcohol/Oxidized sodium alginate nanofibers with fluorescence carbon Dots: Preparation and characterization
coredata.dc:creator: Sattariazar, Simin
Nejad Ebrahimi, Samad
Arsalani, Nasser
coredata.dc:description: The objective of this study was to develop fluorescence nanofibrous polyvinyl alcohol/oxidized sodium alginate (PVA-OSA) incorporated with carbon dots (CDs) through Schiff-base interaction. The carbon dots used in this study were derived from the polyphenol-enriched extract of pomegranate peel, as established in previous work, as the reinforcing and antioxidant agent to enhance the physicochemical and biological properties of the nanofibers were used. The fabricated nanofibers were characterized using FE-SEM, FT-IR, XRD, and DSC analysis. The FE-SEM results revealed that an increase in the number of CDs in the nanofibers led to a decrease in diameter (809.6 ± 77.1 nm to 273.16 ± 41.1 nm). Furthermore, surface modification caused a significant reduction in the amount of surface roughness of the nanofibers. Incorporating CDs not only reduced the scaffold diameter but also improved its mechanical properties and promoted the growth of fibroblast cells. The ultimate tensile strength of scaffolds with and without CDs was 2.15 ± 0.02 MPa and 1.53 ± 0.74 MPa respectively. The influence of CDs amount on the properties of nanofibers showed that the swelling capacity and degradability of nanofibers can be adjusted by changing the range of CDs. Apart from the aforementioned benefits of incorporating CDs in improving nanofiber properties, their exceptional antioxidant properties can be harnessed for protecting nanofibers against oxidation and as a healing agent in wound dressings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Schiff base
Alginate dialdehyde
Electrospun
Nanofiber

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro assessment in contact with (fibroblast cell line)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was funded by Tabriz University of Chemistry, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of different nanofibers A1 (PVA-OSA), A2 (PVA-OSA-CDs 1 mg), A3 (PVA-OSA-CDs 3 mg), A4 (PVA-OSA-CDs 5 mg), A5 (PVA-OSA-CDs 10 mg.ml-1) with the corresponding diameter distribution.
FT-IR spectrum of SA, OSA, PVA-OSA, PVA-OSA-CDs and CDs.
XRD pattern of pristine PVA, OSA-CDs-PVA, OSA-PVA, CDs.
DSC thermogram of PVA-OSA and PVA-OSA-CDs nanofibers scaffold.
Swelling behavior of OSA-PVA and OSA-CDs-PVA scaffolds in different time intervals Biodegradation rate of OSA-PVA and OSA-CDs-PVA with differenr concentration of CDs during 35 days (b).
PVA-OSA and PVA-OSA-CDs stress–strain diagram (a), water contact angle value of of scaffolds.
Distribution of CDs in the PVA-OSA scaffold at different concentration (A1, 0 mg.ml-1), (A2, 1 mg.ml-1),(A3, 3 mg.ml-1), (A4, 5 mg.ml-1) and (A5, 10 mg.ml-1) under fluorescent microscope.
Proliferation evaluation of L929 cultured on scaffolds by MTT assay after 2 days in growth medium. *P < 0.05, ****P < 0.0001.
MTT assay of PVA-OSA nanofiber without incorporating CDs and PVA-OSA-CDs with CDs exposure to different amounts of (t-BHP). ****P < 0.0001.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the properties of electrospun polyvinyl Alcohol/Oxidized sodium alginate nanofibers with fluorescence carbon Dots: Preparation and characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Phytochemistry, Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Phytochemistry, Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The objective of this study was to develop fluorescence nanofibrous polyvinyl alcohol/oxidized sodium alginate (PVA-OSA) incorporated with carbon dots (CDs) through Schiff-base interaction. The carbon dots used in this study were derived from the polyphenol-enriched extract of pomegranate peel, as established in previous work, as the reinforcing and antioxidant agent to enhance the physicochemical and biological properties of the nanofibers were used. The fabricated nanofibers were characterized using FE-SEM, FT-IR, XRD, and DSC analysis. The FE-SEM results revealed that an increase in the number of CDs in the nanofibers led to a decrease in diameter (809.6 ± 77.1 nm to 273.16 ± 41.1 nm). Furthermore, surface modification caused a significant reduction in the amount of surface roughness of the nanofibers. Incorporating CDs not only reduced the scaffold diameter but also improved its mechanical properties and promoted the growth of fibroblast cells. The ultimate tensile strength of scaffolds with and without CDs was 2.15 ± 0.02 MPa and 1.53 ± 0.74 MPa respectively. The influence of CDs amount on the properties of nanofibers showed that the swelling capacity and degradability of nanofibers can be adjusted by changing the range of CDs. Apart from the aforementioned benefits of incorporating CDs in improving nanofiber properties, their exceptional antioxidant properties can be harnessed for protecting nanofibers against oxidation and as a healing agent in wound dressings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The regeneration of damaged tissue and organs through tissue engineering is a complex interdisciplinary endeavor. Various methods, such as self-assembly, phase separation, gas foaming, emulsification, and electrospinning, have been developed to create scaffolds for this purpose. Among these techniques, the electrospinning method has garnered significant interest, as the properties of the resulting nanofiber scaffolds are greatly influenced by the manufacturing process (Alhosseini et al., 2012; Ghorghi et al., 2020). Electrospinning technology allows the construction of scaffolds with a higher surface area than the volume with adjustable porosity and a range of dimensions from nanometers to microns. Another salient feature of nanofibers scaffolds is their great structural similarity to the native extracellular matrix (ECM) to be a promising substrate for tissue engineering applications (Hajiabbas et al., 2020). Electrospinning remains a relatively novel technique, with ongoing research focused on refining the process and uncovering fresh applications for nanofibrous scaffolds. Notably, recent advancements have emerged in the field of electrospinning-based scaffolds, including the creation of radially oriented nanofibers and the integration of conventional electrospinning methods with post-treatment patterns involving hand winding and stretching (Qi et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023). In the electrospinning process, a combination of natural and synthetic polymers can be utilized based on the intended application of the scaffold (Islam and Karim, 2010; Vedhanayagam et al., 2021). Polycaprolactone (PCL), Poly (glycolide) (PGA), polyesters, poly (ethylene oxide) (PEO), and Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) polymers are synthetic polymers due to their biodegradable and biocompatible properties considered as significant candidate materials for wound dressing (Emami et al., 2010; Jafari et al., 2011; Koyyada and Orsu, 2021; Yang et al., 2018). Biopolymers, mainly proteins (gelatin, silk, keratin, fibrin, collagen) and polysaccharides (alginate, chitosan, hyaluronic acid) are non-toxic, biodegradability and biocompatibility have been widely applied for developing skin-regeneration (Pal et al., 2017). Alginate is a linear polysaccharide widely used in the food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industries owing to its capacity to gel and exhibit high viscosity in aqueous solutions, along with its strong biocompatibility. Despite the mentioned advantages, alginate has some limitations such as cell adhesion and penetration and poor mechanical stability, to overcome these issues, studies have focused on the oxidation of alginates (Teng et al., 2021). Modified alginate has more reactive groups, the available aldehyde on oxidized alginate can be coupled by amino groups through Schiff- base reaction (Kim et al., 2012; Kristiansen et al., 2011; Reakasame and Boccaccini, 2018). This leads to the development of composites with increasingly biological performance (Taemeh et al., 2020). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a synthetic polymer that is commonly used to produce nanofibers (Alhosseini et al., 2012). It has a number of advantages for this application, including its biocompatibility, biodegradability, and water solubility. However, it also has some disadvantages, including: low mechanical strength, poor thermal stability, and high water absorption. These disadvantages can be mitigated by using a combination of PVA and other polymers, or by crosslinking the PVA molecules. Several studies on the presence of carbon dots in non-invasive tissue engineering are available, incorporating carbon dots with polymers to develop nanofiber scaffolding to improve electrospun scaffolds' physical and chemical properties (Ghorghi et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). carbon dots have a synergistic effect on scaffolds due to their various properties, such as unique electrical, photoluminescence, and biocompatibility properties. In research by Pal Pallabi et al carbon dots have been utilized to promote fibroblast cell adhesion and proliferation (Pal et al., 2017), in the case of bone tissue Shafiei Sh et al carbon dot performance as osteo-conductively was investigated (Shafiei et al., 2019), Ghorghi et al, showed that the diameter of nanofibers decreased in the presence of carbon dots, followed by an increase in mechanical strength (Ghorghi et al., 2020).

The aim of this study is to design a nanofibrous scaffold to simulate a natural extracellular matrix (ECM) based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/Oxidized sodium alginate (OSA) that is coupled with amino groups of N-doped CDs, which were prepared from polyphenols enriched extract of pomegranate peel. The primary objective of this study is to optimize and eliminate certain disadvantages associated with PVA and SA nanofibers. Accordingly, the mechanical and biological properties of this scaffold have been improved. In this study, CDs are considered multifunctional agents due to their properties. They act as an antioxidant, which leads to an increase in Oxidation Induction Time (OIT). They also act as a therapeutic agent and a reducing agent of nanofiber diameter, which consequently makes it a good candidate for skin tissue engineering.

Sodium meta-periodate (NaIO4), Ethylene Glycol (EG), Dialysis membrane (MWCO = 3KDa) and Sodium Alginate were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). and Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) with a hydrolysis degree of 98–99% purchased from LOBA Feinchemie AG (Austria Chemical Companies).

CDs were synthesized from polyphenol enriched extract following the procedure detailed in our previous work (Sattariazar et al., 2023). In order to synthesize carbon dots (CDs), a two-step process was used which involved preparing an enriched extract of polyphenol compounds from pomegranate peel, followed by synthesizing the CDs using a one-step hydrothermal method. The enriched extract was obtained by washing and drying pomegranate peels, grinding them, and extracting them with ethanol–water. After concentration and purification with ethanol, the extract was mixed with urea, ethylenediamine, citric acid, and PEG 1000, and heated in an autoclave at different temperatures and times. CDs synthesized with ethylenediamine had a higher quantum yield of 20.51%. Optimal conditions for synthesis were determined to be water as a solvent, ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source, a temperature of 180℃, and a reaction time of 8 h. Finally, the CDs were purified using centrifugation and dialysis against distilled water. The produced CDs exhibited an average size of 6 ± 1.2 nm and demonstrated a spherical morphology.

Briefly 3 g sodium alginate (3%) w/v was suspended in 100 ml distilled water, sodium periodate (NaIO4) with 0.6:1 mol ratio to sodium alginate precipitated in water, and then an aqueous solution of sodium periodate was added drop by drop in the dark condition. After 24 h, the oxidation process was stopped by adding ethylene glycol (EG) with an equal mol ratio (1:1) with sodium periodate, and the mixture was dialyzed (MWCO = 3.5 KD) against distilled water for 3 days. The final liquid mixture was lyophilized and stored at −20℃. This procedure was conducted as per the reported technique (Resmi et al., 2020).

The oxidation grade of sodium alginate has been determined by hydroxylamine hydrochloride approach (Zhao and Heindel, 1991), 20 ml of 0.25 N hydroxylamine hydrochloride containing methyl orange was stirred by adding 0.1 g OSA for about 2 h. the resulting mixture was titrated with 0.1 M sodium hydroxide solution until the pH adjusted at 4, which is the endpoint. The consumption volume of sodium hydroxide was recorded and the aldehyde content was computed by the following Equation (1):
OD%= (198 × M
NaOH
(Vsample
-
Vcontrol )/m) × 100.

Where M is the sodium hydroxide molarity, V sample is the consumed amount of sodium hydroxide, the V control is the alkali consumption volume of the blank (blank is the sodium alginate solution at the same concentration of OSA), and m is related to the amount of OSA.

Electrospinning solution was prepared using 2 ml of OSA solution was mixed with different concentrations of CDs (1 mg.ml−1, 3 mg.ml−1, 5 mg.ml−1, 10 mg.ml−1). Afterward the resulting mixture was stirred about 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Next 2 ml of 10% w/v PVA was added to the mixture and the stirring was preserved for another 30 min. the resulting mixture was purred into a 5 ml syringe with a 23G needle, the flow rate adjusted at 1 ml/h by a syringe pump (Top-5300). Electrospinning was carried out with a high voltage of 25 kV, and the nanofiber was collected on an aluminum flat via collector with a 20 rpm speed rate.

The characterization of synthesized CDs discussed in our previous work. Synthesized CDs were characterized for their optical properties, (UV–vis, photoluminescence), and using ATR-FTIR, TEM, FE-SEM, EDX, DLS and Zeta potential analysis. Also, the biological efficiency of the acquired carbon dots was evaluated by citotoxicity, antibacterial, and antioxidant tests. The results showed that the prepared carbon dots have suitable size 6.1 ± 1.2 nm with strong scavenging ability IC50 = 22.18 µg. ml−1. This characteristic renders them desirable for a variety of applications (Sattariazar et al., 2023).

Morphological studies of nanofiber scaffolds were characterized using Field Emission Scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Tescan Mira3), to evaluate the diameter of the nanofiber, Image J software was utilized. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR, THERMO NICOLET) was applied to recognize functional groups and subsequently determine the composition of scaffolds. X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) was carried out to determine the crystalline structure of scaffolds with an X-ray diffractometer (PW 1730, Philips, Netherland), the diffraction patterns were scanned in the range of 10° to 80° range degrees. the thermal behavior of the nanofibers was evaluated by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (Q600, TA, USA) test, in a temperature range between 25 and 600 °C with a 10° min−1 heating rate).

In this experiment the initial weight of the dry nanofibers was measured and recorded as Wd, then swelled into the deionized water and incubated at 37℃. The swollen nanofibers was accurately weighted at interval time (1, 3, 6, 24, 48 h). the swelling value was calculated according to Equation 2:
Swellingratio % =
W w
-
W d
W d
× 100
where (Ww) was the weigth of the wet samples at a predetermined time, and (Wd) was dry nanofibers.

To determine the degradation rate of nanofibers, initially, a specific amount of scaffolds (1 mg) was weighted. Then was soaked into 3 ml buffer saline phosphate at (pH = 7.4) and incubated at 37℃ for up to 4 weeks, samples were taken from the phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at interval times and were eluted with distilled water, left to dry at room temperature for 3hrs and weighed again, the weight loss (%) was calculated by Equation 3:
Weight loss (%)
=
W t
-
W 0
W 0
× 100
where Wt displays samples’ weight after drying and W0 represents the initial weight of samples (Naeimi et al., 2016).

The wettability of nanofibers patches was studied using a contact angle analysis instrument (contact c1, Iran), samples were cut to 1 × 1 cm and the droplet images on the surface of the nanofibers were recorded after 5 s of contact time. The contact angle was measured using image J software.

In order to measure the tensile strength of nanofibers, a SANTAM testing machine (model STM-1, Iran) was applied. Scaffolds were prepared with dimensions of 50 × 10 × 0.02 mm3 and attached from end to pieces of paper to avoid rupturing. Tensile test on the scaffolds were coducted at a speed of 5 mm/min with a load of the cell of 10 N. The Young's modulus of samples was calculated by calculating the slope of the linear region (n = 3) on the stress–strain curve (n = 3).
The distribution of CDs within the scaffold's structure was assessed using a fluorescence microscope (Lesia, TCS SPE, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro assessment in contact with (fibroblast cell line)
ce:section.ce:para: Cell growth and cell viability were quantified using the MTT assay [3-(4, 5-dimehylthiazole-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium Bromide]. In brief, for monolayer culture, cells (L929) were digested with trypsin, harvested, adjusted to a density of 1.4 × 104 cells/well, and seeded to a 96-well plate filled with 200 µl fresh medium per well for 24 h. when cells formed a monolayer, they were exposed to nanofiber scaffolds that were punched into 4 mm circles for 48 h at 37℃ in 5% CO2. Cells treated with nanofiber without the active compound were also included as controls. All the experiments were done in triplicates. At the end of the treatment (48 h), the monolayer culture was left untouched in the original plate, the supernatant was removed and 200 µl/well of MTT solution (0.5 mg/ml in PBS) was added and the plate was incubated at 37℃ for additional 4 h. After 4 h MTT solution (the supernatant of cells was removed and dimethyl sulfoxide was added (100 µl per well). Cells were incubated on a shaker at 37℃ until crystals were completely dissolved. Cell viability was quantified by measuring absorbance at 570 nm using an ELISA reader (Model wave xs2, Bio Tek, USA).

To investigate the ability of nanofibers containing antioxidant substances to preserve fibroblast cells (L929) in the presence of free radicals, (t-BHP) tert-Butyl hydroperoxide was used as oxidative model (Fan et al., 2014; Fan et al., 2012). After sterilization of nanofibers, 2 × 104 fibroblast cells were placed on the surface of nanofibers with a diameter of 5 mm and incubated for 3 days. Then, oxidizing agent with different concentration (0, 100, 200, 400) µm were added to each well and placed in the incubator after 48 h. Eventually, to evaluate cell viability MTT test was carried out following above.

All experiments were repeated at least three times, and the results were presented as the average ± SD (standard deviation). GraphPad Prism 9 software was used to compare the statistical significance between two groups)*P < 0.05, ****P < 0.0001), which was statistically significant.

Fig. 1 shows the morphological histogram and diameter of the nanofibers scaffolding. The dimensions of nanofibers were influenced by the introduction of CDs in varying concentrations to the electrospinning process while maintaining constant parameters such as voltage, collector distance, and injection speed, as illustrated in the Fig. 1. It was observed that the introduction of CDs led to a notable change in the distribution diameter of A1 (PVA-OSA) nanofibers, which increased from 248.7 ± 64.66 nm to 809.6 ± 77.1 nm. However, previous research suggests that the addition of CDs to nanofibers can reduce their diameter due to the electrical conductivity of the CDs (Lee et al., 2016). As the amount of CDs increased in A3, A4, and A5 samples, the average diameter of nanofibers showed a decreasing trend (A3: 443.2 ± 32.46 nm, A4: 300.6 ± 32.46 nm, A5: 273.16 ± 41.1 nm). Notably, SEM images of the PVA-OSA scaffold in group A1 showed surface fragility, which was eliminated after the addition of CDs. This suggests that CDs are an effective additive for enhancing the structural stability of nanofibers.

In Fig. 2 , the FT-IR spectra of nanofibers, oxidized sodium alginate, pristine sodium alginate, CDs and OSA- CDs-PVA are displayed. Previous studies have shown that the peak corresponding to the aldehyde group only appears under normal conditions as it exists in equilibrium with the hemiacetal group in oxidized sodium alginate (OSA). Hence, it is reasonable that there is no absorption peak for the aldehyde group in the FT-IR spectrum (Jejurikar et al., 2012; Mousavi et al., 2021). SA spectrum displays a broad peak at 3299 cm−1 attributed to hydroxyl groups, the stretch vibration carboxylate group appeared at 1654 cm−1. The OSA spectrum exhibits the same peaks of SA with minor shifts and the hydroxyl group peak became slightly weaker than SA. The PVA-OSA nanofiber without CDs shows absorption peaks at 3310 cm−1, 2932 cm−1,1720 cm−1, 1243 cm−1, 1086 cm-1attributing to O–H, C–H, C = O, C-O, and C–C respectively observing the PVA-OSA-CDs spectrum, it can be noted that the addition of CDs to the PVA-OSA nanofiber did not significantly alter the absorption pattern. It was anticipated that the characteristic peak of C = N would appear due to the Schiff-base reaction, but it did not. This could be attributed to either the minimal amount of CDs present in the PVA-OSA matrix or the overlapping of C = N with C = O bands. The broad peaks in the range of 3244–2929 cm−1 at CDs spectrum assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H groups on the surface of CDs. Absorption peak at 1661 and 1567 cm−1, which are respectively attributed to stretching vibration of C = O and C = N groups.

The XRD patterns of pristine PVA, OSA-PVA, OSA-CDs-PVA nanofibers, and CDs within the 2θ range of 10-70° are depicted in Fig. 3 . Based on several studies, the XRD pattern of PVA polymer typically shows a large peak at two angles around 19-20° degrees (2θ) and a smaller peak around 40-41° degrees (2θ). These peaks are indicative of its semicrystalline nature. From the XRD pattern of PVA in Fig. 3, two characteristic peaks can be clearly observed at 2θ = 20.05° and 40.4 (Abdelhamied et al., 2020; Irfan et al., 2021). The distinguished occurrence is the substantial reduction in the intensity of this peak (2θ = 20.05°) in the OSA-PVA pattern, this suggests a reduction in the level of crystallinity within the PVA/OSA blend polymer complex, resulting in a disruption of its crystalline structure. The broad diffraction peak observed at 2θ = 19.54 in the PVA-OSA pattern may be attributed to the strong covalent and hydrogen interactions between the hydroxyl group of PVA and the aldehyde and carboxyl groups of OSA, respectively. These findings align with previous research indicating that the electrospinning process can prevent crystallization and promote the formation of amorphous structures in polymers (wen Jia et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2014). In addition, the CDs pattern revealed the presence of sharp peaks at 2θ = 28.49, 2θ = 31.24, 2θ = 40.69, indicating the crystal structure of CDs, which is consistent with previous studies. after the addition of CDs into the OSA-PVA matrix, the intensity of peak at 2θ = 20.14 with a slight shift (due to intermolecular bonds) has significantly decreased. The study suggests that the CDs, OSA and PVA were effectively tangled within the nanofibers.

The DSC analysis was conducted to investigate the thermal stability and of nanofibers composed of OSA-PVA and OSA-CDs-PVA (Fig. 4 ). According to previous studies, the melting peaks of SA and PVA were approximately 300 and 200 °C, respectively. In addition, a study demonstrated that the combination of SA and PVA had a negative impact on thermal stability (Wang et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2018). Our data showed that the melting point of sodium alginate was raised to 428.58 °C by altering it and incorporating it into polyvinyl alcohol. The increase in active sites in oxidized sodium alginate and the formation of strong covalent bonds with polyvinyl alcohol can be the cause of this phenomenon. Furthermore, the introduction of carbon dots into the structure of OSA-PVA resulted in a decrease in the melting point to 300 °C. This decrease is likely associated with the reduction in the crystallinity of the corresponding nanofibers. The sharp peak appeared at 474.70 °C is due to the removal of some of the stronger hydroxyl groups of the hydrogen bond at the OSA-PVA thermogram. With the addition of CDs, this peak moved towards lower temperatures and became wide. The hydroxyl groups in OSA may form hydrogen bonds with polyvinyl alcohol or carbon dots, which could explain the observed effect.

Fluid uptake is one of the most essential criteria for wound dressing, the physicochemical properties of scaffolds play a significant role on the swelling performance of nanofibers. Fig. 5 (a) showed the swelling ratio of OSA-PVA (a), OSA-CDs-PVA (3 mg) (b), OSA-CDs-PVA (5 mg) (c) and OSA-CDs-PVA (10 mg) (d). According to the findings, a, b, c, and d had an initial swelling capability of 50, 60, 60, and 55% in an hour. After 3 h, all cases showed a rapid and steady decrease in swelling capacity. After that, the de-swelling behavior stopped and reached equilibrium within 48 h. However, the greatest rate of swelling was observed for nanofibres b and c. The density of crosslinking sites could be the cause of the reduced swelling volume in a and d nanofibers. The inter- and intrapolymer reactions created a more rigid network. This has reduced space between polymeric chains, which may also reduce the rate of material swelling. (Amirian et al., 2021).

The nanofiber scaffold's degradation behavior was assessed by immersing it in PBS at 37℃ under specific pH conditions, simulating a physiological environment. The percentage of weight loss trend for PVA-OSA (a) and PVA-OSA-CDs with different concentrations of CDs, namely 3 mg.ml-1 (b), 5 mg.ml-1 (c), and 10 mg.ml-1 (d), was displayed in Fig. 5 (b). The results indicated that the PVA-OSA-CDs (b) scaffold had the highest degradation rate over 35 days, while the PVA-OSA without CDs had the lowest amount of degradation. Overall, the presence of CDs led to an increase in the nanofiber's degradation rate. Interestingly, the scaffolds containing the lowest concentration of CDs exhibited the highest biodegradability rate among those containing carbon dots. This can be explained by the increase in amine groups with an increase in CDs concentration, which enhances the Schiff base ligand's stability, a phenomenon that has been previously reported in other studies. (Amirian et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020). The results indicated that the presence of CDs led to an increase in the degradation rate of the nanofiber scaffolds. This finding is significant since it suggests that CDs can be utilized to enhance the biodegradability of nanofiber scaffolds, making them more suitable for various biomedical applications. This finding is consistent with previous studies, which further supports the credibility of the present study's results.

The results of the mechanical strength test are presented in Table 1 and Fig. 6 (a). The data shows that the ultimate tensile strength and Young's modulus of PVA-OSA nanofibers were enhanced after the incorporation of CDs. The stress–strain plot demonstrated that the elasticity and strength properties of the nanofibers were improved simultaneously. The addition of CDs to the nanofibers through a Schiff-base interaction with the aldehyde groups of OSA formed strong connections, resulting in increased strength of the nanofibers.The study suggests that incorporating CDs into PVA-OSA nanofibers can improve their mechanical properties. The Schiff-base interaction between CDs and OSA plays a vital role in creating strong connections and enhancing the strength of the nanofibers. The findings of this study have important implications for the development of high-performance nanofibers with enhanced mechanical properties.

Fig. 6 (b) illustrates the results of the wettability analysis. In tissue engineering, the adhesion of cells and proteins to the surface of polymers is critical, and hydrophobicity and hydrophilicity are two essential factors in controlling this adhesion. The water contact angle of PVA-OSA and PVA-OSA-CDs was found to be 78.89°±3.72 and 84.47°±2.01, respectively. Although the addition of CDs increased the wettable angle, the change was not significant. It is expected that the contact angle would decrease due to the hydrophilic nature of CDs. However, the increase in covalent crosslinking of ADA with amine groups and hydroxyl groups, as well as the formation of acetal ring linkage, can explain this phenomenon.(Narayanan et al., 2020). The study suggests that the addition of CDs to PVA-OSA nanofibers increases the water contact angle, but the change is not significant. This finding can be explained by the increased covalent crosslinking of ADA with amine and hydroxyl groups, as well as the formation of acetal ring linkage. The results of this study have important implications for the development of nanofibers with desired wettability properties for tissue engineering applications.

The fluorescence microscopy analysis presented in Fig. 7 illustrates the dispersion of carbon dots (CDs) in a nanofiber scaffold at varying concentrations using their emission properties under UV light. This examination demonstrates that CDs confer fluorescent characteristics to the scaffolds and that the emission intensity of the fluorescence increases as the concentration of CDs increases, The figure shows how the distribution of CDs affects the fluorescent properties of the nanofiber scaffold. As the concentration of CDs increases, the scaffold becomes more fluorescent, and this effect is more pronounced when excited with light at 360 nm. This information is important for understanding how the properties of the scaffold can be tuned by altering the concentration of CDs, which can have implications in various applications such as sensing, imaging and medical diagnosis.

The study evaluated the impact of PVA-OSA and PVA-OSA-CDs (10 mg) nanofiber scaffolds on the viability of L929 fibroblast cells using the MTT test. The results presented in Fig. 8 indicate that after 48 h of incubation, the cell viability on the PVA-OSA scaffold decreased compared to the PVA-OSA-CDs scaffold. These findings suggest that PVA-OSA-CDs nanofiber scaffolds have the potential to promote cell proliferation and are non-toxic. The antioxidant properties and high biocompatibility of CDs appear to contribute to this characteristic of nanofibers, making them suitable for wound dressings. The results showed that the PVA-OSA-CDs scaffold was better at promoting cell proliferation and was non-toxic, likely due to the antioxidant properties and biocompatibility of CDs.

Oxidative stress occurs when the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is imbalanced with the body's ability to remove them. This imbalance can lead to a number of harmful effects on tissues and cells, including loss of cell integration, metabolic abnormalities, diverted enzyme activity, and genomic imbalances. Therefore, it is expected that nanofibers containing CDs, which have high radical scavenging properties, will have a higher survival rate of L929 fibroblast cells than nanofibers without CDs when exposed to different amounts of t-BHP. Fig. 9 shows the results of an investigation into the survival of fibroblast cells under oxidative conditions in the presence of different amounts of t-BHP using the MTT assay. The results indicate that the cell viability of cells in PVA-OSA-CDs nanofibers is significantly higher than that in PVA-OSA nanofibers. Given that the CDs used in this study have high antioxidant scavenging properties, these results confirm the beneficial effects of CDs on cell survival under oxidative stress conditions.

Electrospinning technology was used to produce PVA-OSA and PVA-OSA-CDs nanocomposites. The incorporation of CDs into the nanofibers not only enhanced mechanical properties, cell proliferation, and biodegradability but also had a significant impact on the surface fragility. As the concentration of carbon dots increased, the surface of the nanofibers became smooth and uniform. Additionally, the CDs possessed excellent antioxidant properties, which could provide anti-inflammatory effects to the scaffolds, offering potential applications in wound dressing and other biomedical areas. The study emphasizes the potential of CDs to improve the biodegradability of nanofiber scaffolds, which could have significant implications in the fields of tissue engineering and regenerative medicine. Further research is necessary to investigate the underlying mechanisms and optimize the use of CDs for this purpose. In summary, the study demonstrated that PVA-OSA-CDs nanofiber scaffolds have the potential to promote cell proliferation, are non-toxic, and offer improved cell survival under oxidative conditions. These benefits are likely due to the high biocompatibility and antioxidant properties of CDs, indicating their potential use in wound dressing and other biomedical applications.

Simin Sattariazar: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Samad Nejad Ebrahimi: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Review & editing original draft. Nasser Arsalani: Supervision, Review & editing original draft.
The present work was funded by Tabriz University of Chemistry, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The University of Tabriz financially supported this work.
